old_claim.note: Open Source Software provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Continuous Integration requires a shared version-controlled mainline or controlled source repository so changes can be integrated, rebuilt, and traced.

source_shape.source_locator: Fowler lists putting everything in a version-controlled mainline as a CI practice and states that CI relies on a clear mainline.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source identifies a version-controlled mainline or controlled source repository as part of the CI method.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports replacing the open-source edge because CI needs source revision control and a shared integration branch, not a public licensing or community-development model.

ontology_before: Continuous Integration depended on Open Source Software, conflating source availability/licensing and community workflow with the source-control mechanism CI actually uses.
ontology_after: Continuous Integration depends on Version Control Systems for the shared mainline and repository needed to integrate, rebuild, and trace changes.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: continuous_integration now depends on version_control_systems and no longer directly depends on open_source_software.
would_reject_if: The Continuous Integration node were narrowed to open-source project hosting workflows only.
A stronger source showed CI requires open-source licensing or public community maintenance rather than version control.
The version_control_systems node were narrowed away from shared source-code repositories and mainlines.
short_reason: CI requires version control, not open-source licensing.
